enrichment.short_description: Valkey is a free, open-source, in-memory key-value data store forked from Redis in 2024 and governed by the Linux Foundation, used for caching, message queues, and as a primary database.
enrichment.company_size: Open-source project under the Linux Foundation, with roughly 50 contributing companies
enrichment.seo_title: Valkey: The Open-Source Redis Fork Explained
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Valkey is a free, BSD-licensed, Linux Foundation-backed fork of Redis for caching, messaging, and data storage. See features, history, and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Valkey is an open-source, in-memory key-value data store used for caching, message queuing, and as a primary database, functioning as a drop-in replacement for Redis. It was created in March 2024 after Redis Inc. changed the licensing of Redis from the permissive BSD 3-Clause license to the source-available RSALv2 and SSPLv1 dual license starting with Redis 7.4, a move that was not recognized as open source by the Open Source Initiative. In response, the Linux Foundation announced Valkey as a community-governed fork built on the last BSD-licensed Redis codebase, version 7.2.4. Six founding maintainers from six different companies led the fork: Zhao Zhao (Alibaba), BinBin Zhu (Tencent), Viktor Soderqvist (Ericsson), Lucas Yang (Huawei), Ping Xie (Oracle, ex-Google), and Madelyn Olson (Amazon). Within its first year, Valkey grew to nearly 50 contributing companies, including AWS, Google Cloud, Oracle, ByteDance, Alibaba Cloud, Percona, Aiven, and DigitalOcean, and surpassed thousands of commits, 150-plus contributors, and millions of Docker pulls. Valkey supports the familiar Redis data structures, including strings, hashes, lists, sets, sorted sets, bitmaps, and hyperloglogs, along with clustering, replication, Lua scripting, and a module system for extensibility. Because it remains BSD 3-Clause licensed, it is free to self-host, embed, and redistribute without the license restrictions that apply to newer Redis versions. Valkey competes directly with Redis, including Redis's own AGPLv3 open-source option introduced with Redis 8, as well as managed alternatives like AWS ElastiCache for Valkey, Google Cloud Memorystore, Upstash, and Aiven for Valkey. Its main draw is licensing certainty backed by neutral foundation governance rather than a single vendor, making it attractive to cloud providers and enterprises wary of future license changes.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Valkey
paragraphs: Valkey is a free and open-source, high-performance, in-memory key-value data store that can be used for caching, message queuing, session storage, and as a general-purpose primary database. It was designed as a compatible, drop-in alternative to Redis, supporting the same core commands, data structures, and client libraries that developers already use.

The project was launched in March 2024 in direct response to Redis Inc.'s decision to move Redis away from its original BSD 3-Clause open-source license toward a dual RSALv2/SSPLv1 source-available license. A group of engineers from AWS, Google, Oracle, Ericsson, Alibaba, Tencent, and Huawei forked the last BSD-licensed release of Redis, version 7.2.4, and placed the new project under the neutral governance of the Linux Foundation.

Unlike a vendor-controlled project, Valkey is steered by a technical steering committee drawn from multiple competing companies, which the Linux Foundation says guarantees the codebase will remain open source permanently. This governance model has made Valkey the default choice for several major cloud providers migrating their managed Redis-compatible services.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Valkey retains full compatibility with the Redis data model, supporting strings, hashes, lists, sets, sorted sets, streams, bitmaps, and hyperloglogs, so most existing Redis clients, drivers, and tooling work against Valkey with no code changes.
It supports standalone, replicated, and clustered deployment modes, giving teams options for both simple caching layers and horizontally scaled, highly available deployments across many nodes.

Valkey includes built-in Lua scripting for atomic server-side operations, a module system for extending the server with custom commands and data types, and ongoing performance work such as multi-threaded I/O aimed at improving throughput over the pre-fork Redis baseline.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Valkey itself is completely free. As a BSD 3-Clause licensed open-source project, it can be downloaded, self-hosted, modified, and redistributed, including in commercial products, without license fees or usage restrictions.

Most organizations pay only for the infrastructure running Valkey, or for a managed version offered by a cloud vendor, such as AWS ElastiCache for Valkey, Google Cloud Memorystore for Valkey, Aiven for Valkey, or Percona's distribution, each of which prices Valkey as a managed database service on top of the free open-source core.

features.title: Redis-Compatible Data Structures
features.description: Supports strings, hashes, lists, sets, sorted sets, streams, bitmaps, and hyperloglogs with the same commands used by Redis clients.

features.title: Cluster Mode
features.description: Horizontally scales across many nodes with automatic sharding, replication, and failover for high-availability deployments.

features.title: Lua Scripting
features.description: Runs atomic server-side scripts to combine multiple operations without round trips to the client.

features.title: Module System
features.description: Extends the server with custom commands and data types through a C-based module API compatible with the Redis Modules API.

features.title: Multi-Threaded I/O
features.description: Optional multi-threading for network I/O improves throughput on multi-core hosts compared to the traditional single-threaded event loop.

features.title: Replication and Persistence
features.description: Supports asynchronous replication plus RDB snapshotting and append-only file persistence for durability.

features.title: BSD 3-Clause License
features.description: Fully permissive open-source license with no source-available restrictions, usable freely in commercial and embedded products.

features.title: Linux Foundation Governance
features.description: Maintained by a multi-vendor technical steering committee rather than a single company, reducing the risk of future license changes.

pros: Free, permissively licensed (BSD 3-Clause) with no usage restrictions
Drop-in compatible with existing Redis clients and tooling
Backed by major cloud providers including AWS, Google Cloud, and Oracle
Neutral, multi-vendor governance under the Linux Foundation reduces vendor lock-in risk
Active development with frequent releases and a growing contributor base
cons: Younger project than Redis, with a shorter track record in production at scale
Some newer Redis features released after the fork may need to be reimplemented separately
Requires self-hosting expertise unless using a managed provider
Ecosystem tooling built specifically around Redis branding sometimes lags Valkey support

faqs.question: What is Valkey?
faqs.answer: Valkey is a free, open-source, in-memory key-value data store forked from Redis in March 2024, used for caching, message queuing, and as a primary database, and governed by the Linux Foundation.

faqs.question: Why was Valkey created?
faqs.answer: Valkey was created after Redis Inc. changed Redis's license from the permissive BSD 3-Clause license to the source-available RSALv2 and SSPLv1 dual license, which is not recognized as open source. A group of engineers forked the last BSD-licensed version, Redis 7.2.4, to keep a truly open-source option available.

faqs.question: Is Valkey free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. Valkey is licensed under BSD 3-Clause, so it can be downloaded, self-hosted, modified, and used commercially at no cost.

faqs.question: Is Valkey compatible with Redis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Valkey supports the same data structures, commands, and most client libraries as Redis 7.2, so existing Redis applications generally work with Valkey without code changes.

faqs.question: Who governs Valkey?
faqs.answer: Valkey is governed by the Linux Foundation through a technical steering committee made up of engineers from multiple companies, including AWS, Google, Oracle, Ericsson, Alibaba, Tencent, and Huawei.

faqs.question: Which cloud providers support Valkey?
faqs.answer: AWS, Google Cloud, Oracle Cloud, Aiven, Percona, and DigitalOcean, among others, offer managed Valkey services or actively contribute to the project.

faqs.question: How is Valkey different from Redis 8's open-source AGPLv3 option?
faqs.answer: Redis later added AGPLv3 as an open-source licensing option with Redis 8, but Valkey remains under the more permissive BSD 3-Clause license and continues to be developed independently under Linux Foundation governance rather than by Redis Inc.

use_cases.title: Application Caching
use_cases.description: Teams use Valkey as a fast in-memory cache in front of relational or document databases to reduce latency and database load.

use_cases.title: Session Storage and Message Queuing
use_cases.description: Valkey stores user session data and powers lightweight pub/sub and queuing patterns for web and mobile applications.

use_cases.title: Redis Migration for License Compliance
use_cases.description: Organizations that need a permissively licensed, drop-in Redis alternative migrate existing Redis deployments to Valkey to avoid RSALv2/SSPLv1 licensing terms.
use_cases.audience: Platform and infrastructure teams at cloud vendors and enterprises

features: Community support via GitHub and Slack
Fully managed hosting (e.g. AWS ElastiCache for Valkey, Google Memorystore, Aiven)

enrichment.short_description: Vanta is a security and compliance automation platform that continuously monitors a company's systems to help it achieve and maintain certifications such as SOC 2, ISO 27001, HIPAA, and GDPR.
enrichment.seo_title: Vanta: Automated Security and Compliance Platform
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Vanta automates SOC 2, ISO 27001, HIPAA, and GDPR compliance with continuous monitoring, evidence collection, and AI-assisted questionnaires.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Vanta is a trust and compliance management platform that automates the manual work of achieving and maintaining security certifications and regulatory compliance. Founded in 2018 by Christina Cacioppo and Erik Goldman, both former Dropbox employees, Vanta went through Y Combinator before growing into one of the leading players in the compliance automation category, alongside competitors like Drata, Secureframe, and Sprinto. The company is headquartered in San Francisco, California, with additional hubs in New York, Dublin, and Sydney, and employs roughly 700 people. Vanta connects to a company's cloud infrastructure, HR systems, version control, and other tools through more than 400 integrations to continuously monitor security controls, automatically collect audit evidence, and flag compliance gaps in real time, rather than relying on the point-in-time spreadsheet audits that were previously standard. It supports frameworks including SOC 2, ISO 27001, HIPAA, GDPR, PCI DSS, HITRUST, ISO 42001, NIST AI RMF, FedRAMP, and CMMC, and also offers third-party vendor risk management, a public-facing Trust Center for sharing security posture with prospects, and AI-assisted drafting of security questionnaire responses. As of 2026 Vanta reports serving more than 16,000 customers, including Ramp, Cursor, Snowflake, Duolingo, GitHub, and Samsara, and has raised over 500 million dollars in funding, most recently a 150 million dollar Series D in mid-2025 that valued the company at roughly 4.15 billion dollars. Pricing is not publicly listed; third-party sources estimate annual contracts typically range from about 10,000 dollars for smaller Core plans up to 80,000 dollars or more for Scale and Enterprise tiers, with the median deal size cited around 19,000 dollars per year, plus optional add-ons such as vendor risk management. Vanta differs from application security testing tools like Veracode by focusing on organizational compliance and trust posture rather than scanning source code for vulnerabilities.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Vanta
paragraphs: Vanta is a compliance and trust management platform built to automate the evidence-gathering and monitoring work required to earn and maintain security certifications like SOC 2 and ISO 27001. Instead of manually screenshotting settings and chasing down access logs before an audit, companies connect their cloud accounts, identity provider, version control system, and HR tools to Vanta, which then continuously checks those systems against the controls required by each framework.

The company was founded in San Francisco in 2018 by Christina Cacioppo and Erik Goldman, two former Dropbox employees who built the product after going through Y Combinator. Vanta emerged during a wave of high-profile data breaches, when startups and mid-market companies were increasingly being asked by enterprise customers to prove their security posture before signing a contract.

Today Vanta serves more than 16,000 customers, from early-stage startups to large enterprises, and is used by companies such as Ramp, Cursor, Snowflake, Clay, Duolingo, GitHub, and Samsara. It has raised over 500 million dollars in venture funding and was valued at roughly 4.15 billion dollars following a 150 million dollar Series D round in 2025.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Vanta's core product continuously monitors cloud infrastructure, code repositories, HR platforms, and other business systems through more than 400 pre-built integrations, automatically flagging misconfigurations or missing controls that would otherwise be caught only during a manual audit.

The platform maps a company's existing controls to the requirements of multiple frameworks at once, including SOC 2, ISO 27001, HIPAA, GDPR, PCI DSS, HITRUST, ISO 42001, NIST AI RMF, FedRAMP, and CMMC, so teams pursuing more than one certification do not have to duplicate evidence collection.

Newer AI-assisted features include an in-product assistant that helps draft security policies, summarize risks, and auto-complete customer security questionnaires, which Vanta says can cut manual questionnaire response time significantly. It also offers a public Trust Center that lets sales and security teams share real-time compliance status with prospective customers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Vanta does not publish its pricing publicly and instead sells through custom, quote-based annual contracts negotiated by its sales team. Third-party pricing trackers estimate that a Core-tier plan starts around 10,000 dollars per year, with Plus, Growth, Scale, and Enterprise tiers rising toward 80,000 dollars or more per year based on company size, number of frameworks, and add-ons.

Common add-ons cited by buyers include a separate vendor risk management module priced around 11,000 dollars per year and framework-specific modules. Buyers are advised to budget for external audit fees separately, since Vanta automates evidence collection but does not itself replace an independent auditor's report.

features.title: Continuous Control Monitoring
features.description: Automatically checks cloud, identity, and code systems against framework requirements around the clock rather than only before an audit.

features.title: 400+ Integrations
features.description: Connects to AWS, GCP, Azure, GitHub, Okta, Google Workspace, and hundreds of other tools to pull compliance evidence automatically.

features.title: Multi-Framework Support
features.description: Maps one set of controls to SOC 2, ISO 27001, HIPAA, GDPR, PCI DSS, and other frameworks simultaneously.

features.title: Vendor Risk Management
features.description: Monitors the security posture of third-party vendors and suppliers a company depends on.

features.title: Trust Center
features.description: A public, shareable page that displays a company's live security and compliance status to prospects and customers.

features.title: AI Questionnaire Automation
features.description: Uses AI to draft responses to inbound security questionnaires from prospective customers.

features.title: Audit Readiness and Evidence Collection
features.description: Organizes and timestamps evidence for external auditors, streamlining the SOC 2 or ISO 27001 audit process.

features.title: Risk Register
features.description: Centralizes and tracks identified risks and remediation tasks across the organization.

pros: Significantly reduces the manual work of compliance evidence collection through automation
Supports many frameworks from a single connected control set
Large integration library covering most common cloud and SaaS tools
Well-regarded by analysts, named a Leader in Forrester's GRC Platforms Wave for Q2 2026
Strong brand recognition that customers and auditors are familiar with
cons: Pricing is opaque and requires a sales conversation, which can be a friction point for small teams
Annual contracts and add-ons can get expensive as a company scales frameworks and vendors
Automated monitoring does not replace the cost of the independent audit itself
Initial setup and control mapping still requires meaningful internal time investment

faqs.question: What is Vanta used for?
faqs.answer: Vanta is used to automate the monitoring, evidence collection, and readiness work required to achieve and maintain security certifications such as SOC 2, ISO 27001, HIPAA, and GDPR.

faqs.question: How much does Vanta cost?
faqs.answer: Vanta uses custom, quote-based annual pricing that is not published publicly. Industry estimates put entry-level plans around 10,000 dollars per year, scaling toward 80,000 dollars or more for larger Enterprise deployments.

faqs.question: Who founded Vanta?
faqs.answer: Vanta was founded in 2018 by Christina Cacioppo and Erik Goldman, both former Dropbox employees, through Y Combinator.

faqs.question: Does Vanta replace an auditor?
faqs.answer: No. Vanta automates evidence collection and continuous monitoring, but companies still need to engage an independent, accredited auditor to issue the official SOC 2 report or ISO 27001 certification.

faqs.question: What frameworks does Vanta support?
faqs.answer: Vanta supports SOC 2, ISO 27001, HIPAA, GDPR, PCI DSS, HITRUST, ISO 42001, NIST AI RMF, FedRAMP, CMMC, and other frameworks.

faqs.question: Is Vanta only for startups?
faqs.answer: No. While Vanta is popular with startups going through their first SOC 2 audit, it also serves large enterprises, and its customer base includes companies like Snowflake and Samsara.

faqs.question: How is Vanta different from Veracode?
faqs.answer: Vanta automates organizational security and compliance monitoring for certifications like SOC 2, while Veracode is an application security testing company that scans source code and applications for vulnerabilities. The two solve different problems and are often used together.

use_cases.title: First SOC 2 Audit for Startups
use_cases.description: Early-stage companies use Vanta to stand up security controls and collect audit evidence ahead of their first SOC 2 Type I or Type II report, often required to close enterprise sales deals.
use_cases.audience: Startup founders and first security hires

use_cases.title: Multi-Framework Compliance at Scale
use_cases.description: Mid-market and enterprise security teams use Vanta to manage overlapping requirements across SOC 2, ISO 27001, HIPAA, and GDPR from a single control set.

use_cases.title: Vendor Risk Reviews
use_cases.description: Procurement and security teams use Vanta's vendor risk module to assess and continuously monitor the security posture of third-party suppliers.

features: Dedicated support and advanced AI features

enrichment.short_description: Vector is a free, open-source, high-performance observability data pipeline for collecting, transforming, and routing logs, metrics, and traces, originally built by Timber.io and now maintained by Datadog.
enrichment.company_size: Maintained by Datadog's Community Open Source Engineering team; Datadog has 5,000+ employees
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Vector is a free, Rust-built, open-source tool for collecting, transforming, and routing logs and metrics, maintained by Datadog. Not a vector database.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Vector is a free, open-source observability data pipeline tool used to collect, transform, and route logs, metrics, and traces between systems, functioning as a vendor-neutral alternative to agents like Logstash, Fluentd, or Fluent Bit. It should not be confused with a vector database used for AI embeddings and similarity search; despite the similar name, Vector is purely a data-pipeline and log-shipping tool with no relation to vector search technology. The project began with its first commits in 2018 at Timber.io, a startup building developer-facing logging tools. Timber.io open-sourced Vector as a lightweight, memory-safe alternative to older log shippers, emphasizing that it was built in Rust for speed and low resource usage, capable of running as a daemon, sidecar, or aggregator across cloud and on-premise environments. In February 2021, Datadog, the publicly traded observability and monitoring company, acquired Timber Technologies specifically to bring Vector into its portfolio, and Vector has since been maintained by Datadog's Community Open Source Engineering team while remaining fully open source and vendor-neutral in the destinations it supports. Vector ships as a single static binary with no external runtime dependencies, and as of recent releases supports dozens of sources, such as Kafka, Kubernetes, and Syslog, transforms for filtering and enriching data, and sinks for routing to destinations including Datadog, Elasticsearch, S3, Splunk, and Prometheus, among many others. The project has attracted a large open-source community, with tens of thousands of GitHub stars, hundreds of contributors, and tens of millions of downloads reported across more than 40 countries. Because Datadog does not restrict Vector to its own platform, teams use it as a cost-efficient, high-throughput layer for building observability pipelines regardless of which monitoring backend they ultimately send data to, competing with tools such as Fluent Bit, Logstash, OpenTelemetry Collector, and Cribl.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Vector
paragraphs: Vector is an open-source data pipeline built specifically for observability data: logs, metrics, and traces. It sits between the systems producing telemetry, such as application servers, containers, and cloud services, and the destinations that store or analyze that data, giving teams a single tool to collect, filter, transform, and route everything.

The project traces back to Timber.io, a startup that originally built developer-friendly logging products before shifting focus to build Vector as an open-source infrastructure tool. Its first commits date to 2018, and it was designed from the start in Rust for speed, memory safety, and minimal resource overhead compared to older, JVM-based or interpreted log shippers.

In February 2021, Datadog acquired Timber Technologies specifically to bring Vector's technology and team in-house. Vector has remained open source since the acquisition and continues to support routing data to many destinations beyond Datadog itself, and it is now maintained by Datadog's dedicated open-source engineering team.

It is important not to confuse Vector with a vector database. Vector.dev has no connection to AI embeddings, similarity search, or retrieval-augmented generation; it is strictly a logs-and-metrics pipeline tool, a distinct category from vector databases like Pinecone, Weaviate, or Milvus.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Vector supports dozens of sources for ingesting data, including Kubernetes logs, Kafka, Syslog, cloud provider log streams, and application-level log files, along with dozens of sinks for sending processed data onward to destinations like Datadog, Elasticsearch, Splunk, S3, Loki, Prometheus, and Kafka.

Its transform layer lets teams parse, filter, sample, aggregate, and enrich log and metric data in flight using the Vector Remap Language (VRL), reducing the volume and cost of data sent to expensive downstream storage.

Vector deploys as a single, dependency-free binary that can run as a lightweight agent on each host, a sidecar in a container, or a centralized aggregator, and its Rust foundation gives it high throughput with a comparatively small memory and CPU footprint versus older JVM-based shippers like Logstash.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Vector is entirely free and open source. There is no paid tier, seat-based pricing, or usage cap on the software itself, since it is a self-hosted pipeline component rather than a hosted SaaS product.

Organizations pay only for the compute resources needed to run Vector and, separately, for whatever destination platforms they route data into, such as Datadog itself, Elasticsearch, or a cloud storage bucket, each of which has its own independent pricing.

features.title: Multi-Source Ingestion
features.description: Collects logs and metrics from dozens of sources including Kubernetes, Kafka, Syslog, and cloud log services.

features.title: Vector Remap Language (VRL)
features.description: A purpose-built expression language for parsing, filtering, and transforming events without writing custom code.

features.title: Wide Sink Support
features.description: Routes processed data to dozens of destinations, from Datadog and Elasticsearch to S3, Splunk, and Prometheus.

features.title: Single Static Binary
features.description: Ships as one dependency-free binary that runs as an agent, sidecar, or aggregator with no runtime installation required.

features.title: High Throughput, Low Overhead
features.description: Built in Rust for memory safety and performance, competing on speed with older log shippers like Logstash.

features.title: Vendor-Neutral Routing
features.description: Not locked to any single observability backend, letting teams change destinations without re-architecting pipelines.

features.title: Open-Source and Free
features.description: Fully open source with an active community, with no license fees for the software itself.

pros: Completely free and open source with no vendor lock-in on destinations
High performance and low resource usage thanks to its Rust foundation
Broad connector library covering most common observability sources and sinks
Backed by Datadog engineering resources while remaining vendor-neutral
Reduces observability costs by filtering and shaping data before it reaches paid storage
cons: Configuration and VRL have a learning curve for teams new to pipeline tools
Being maintained by a competitor's parent company can raise questions for shops standardized on other vendors
Requires self-hosting and operational ownership, unlike a fully managed SaaS pipeline
Less turnkey than simpler single-purpose agents for teams with very basic logging needs

faqs.question: What is Vector (vector.dev)?
faqs.answer: Vector is a free, open-source tool for collecting, transforming, and routing observability data such as logs, metrics, and traces between systems and destinations.

faqs.question: Is Vector a vector database?
faqs.answer: No. Despite the name, Vector is not a vector database or AI embeddings store. It is an observability data pipeline tool, unrelated to vector search technologies like Pinecone or Milvus.

faqs.question: Who created Vector?
faqs.answer: Vector was created by Timber.io, with development starting in 2018, and has been maintained by Datadog's open-source engineering team since Datadog acquired Timber Technologies in February 2021.

faqs.question: Is Vector free?
faqs.answer: Yes. Vector is fully open source and free to self-host, with no license fees for the software itself.

faqs.question: Does Vector only work with Datadog?
faqs.answer: No. Vector is vendor-neutral and can route data to many destinations, including Elasticsearch, Splunk, S3, Prometheus, Loki, and Kafka, in addition to Datadog.

faqs.question: What language is Vector written in?
faqs.answer: Vector is written in Rust, which gives it strong performance and memory safety compared with older, interpreted or JVM-based log shippers.

faqs.question: How does Vector compare to Fluent Bit or Logstash?
faqs.answer: Vector competes directly with Fluent Bit, Logstash, and the OpenTelemetry Collector as a pipeline for logs and metrics, generally emphasizing higher throughput and a unified configuration model for both logs and metrics.

use_cases.title: Reducing Observability Costs
use_cases.description: Platform teams use Vector to filter, sample, and shape high-volume log data before it reaches expensive storage or SaaS observability platforms, cutting ingestion costs.

use_cases.title: Unifying Multi-Cloud Log Pipelines
use_cases.description: Organizations running workloads across multiple clouds use Vector as a single agent to normalize and route logs and metrics from many sources into one or more backends.

use_cases.title: Migrating Off Legacy Log Shippers
use_cases.description: Teams replace older JVM-based tools like Logstash with Vector to reduce resource usage and simplify pipeline configuration.

features: Full source and sink connector library
Community support via GitHub and Discord
Self-hosted deployment as agent, sidecar, or aggregator

enrichment.short_description: Vectr is a browser-based vector graphics editor and AI logo maker with real-time collaboration, letting users create and export SVG artwork for free or on paid plans with added AI tools.
enrichment.company_size: Small team under Pixlr / Inmagine Group
enrichment.headquarters: Singapore (Pixlr Pte Ltd, part of Inmagine Group)
enrichment.seo_title: Vectr: Free Browser-Based Vector Graphics Editor
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Vectr is a free, browser-based vector graphics and logo design tool with real-time collaboration, SVG export, and optional AI-powered features.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Vectr is a browser-based (and formerly desktop) vector graphics editor aimed at beginners and small teams who need a simple, collaborative tool for creating logos, icons, and other SVG artwork without the complexity of professional software like Adobe Illustrator. The product was originally built by Vectr Labs, a startup founded around 2014 by a designer-and-developer pair who wanted a cross-platform, collaborative vector editor; the company raised roughly 910,000 dollars in venture funding, led by Resolute Ventures, and launched out of beta in September 2016. In November 2017, Vectr Labs was acquired by 123RF, a stock media company, and the product has since operated under Pixlr Pte Ltd, a Singapore-based company within the Inmagine Group, which also owns 123RF and the Pixlr photo editor, reflecting a common thread of design and stock-content tools under one corporate umbrella. Vectr's core feature set includes real-time multi-user collaboration with built-in chat, cross-platform syncing between web and desktop, an AI logo maker, image-to-vector (raster-to-SVG) conversion, and export to SVG, PNG, and JPG formats. In more recent years the product has added generative AI tools such as background removal, text-to-vector generation, image enhancement, and object removal, aligning it with the broader shift among lightweight design tools toward AI-assisted workflows. Vectr has historically been positioned as a free alternative to paid vector editors, though it has moved toward a freemium model: a free tier offers limited storage (around 30 MB) and includes ads, while paid Plus and Premium tiers, priced roughly in the 4 to 10 dollar per month range, add more storage, AI credit allowances, and priority support. It competes with other lightweight or free vector tools such as Inkscape, Canva's vector features, and Figma for simple design tasks, though it does not offer the depth of a professional illustration tool like Adobe Illustrator or Affinity Designer.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Vectr
paragraphs: Vectr is a vector graphics editor that runs in the browser and, historically, as a downloadable desktop app, designed to make basic vector design and logo creation approachable for non-designers. It positions itself as a simpler, more collaborative alternative to professional tools like Adobe Illustrator.

The product originated at Vectr Labs, a startup founded around 2014 by a small team combining design and engineering backgrounds, with the explicit goal of building a cross-platform, real-time collaborative vector editor. The company raised about 910,000 dollars in venture funding, led by Resolute Ventures, and took the product out of beta in September 2016.

Vectr Labs was acquired by stock media company 123RF in November 2017. The product is now operated under Pixlr Pte Ltd, part of Singapore-based Inmagine Group, which also owns the Pixlr photo editor and 123RF stock photo marketplace, placing Vectr within a broader family of creative and stock-content tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Vectr's signature feature is real-time collaboration: multiple users can edit the same vector document simultaneously and chat inside the editor, similar in spirit to collaborative document editors but applied to vector graphics.

The tool includes an AI logo maker for quickly generating logo concepts, an image-to-vector converter that turns raster JPG or PNG images into editable SVG paths, and newer generative AI add-ons such as background removal, text-to-vector generation, and object removal.

Projects sync automatically across the web app and any connected devices, and designs can be exported in SVG, PNG, JPG, and Vectr's native file format, with commercial usage rights included even on lower-tier plans.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Vectr offers a free plan with around 30 MB of storage, a limited element library, and ads, aimed at casual or one-off use. Paid plans remove some of these limits and add AI credits.

The Plus plan is priced around 4 dollars per month with roughly 1 GB of storage and a monthly allotment of AI credits, while the Premium plan is priced around 9.99 dollars per month with about 5 GB of storage, a larger AI credit allowance, support for multiple concurrent sessions, and priority support.

features.title: Real-Time Collaboration
features.description: Multiple users can edit the same vector file simultaneously with built-in chat.

features.title: AI Logo Maker
features.description: Generates logo concepts and starting templates using AI.

features.title: Image-to-Vector Conversion
features.description: Converts raster JPG or PNG images into editable SVG vector paths.

features.title: Cross-Platform Sync
features.description: Keeps projects in sync between the browser-based editor and connected devices.

features.title: SVG, PNG, and JPG Export
features.description: Exports finished designs in standard vector and raster formats.

features.title: Generative AI Tools
features.description: Includes background removal, text-to-vector generation, and object removal powered by AI.

features.title: Large Element Library
features.description: Provides access to tens of thousands of pre-made design elements and templates.

pros: Free tier is genuinely usable for simple vector design and logo work
Real-time collaboration is unusual among free vector tools
Low learning curve compared to professional vector software
Works entirely in the browser with no install required
Commercial usage rights included even on lower-tier plans
cons: Far less powerful than professional tools like Adobe Illustrator or Affinity Designer for complex illustration work
Free plan storage is limited and includes ads
AI features are metered by credits that can run out quickly on lower plans
Smaller and less frequently updated compared to larger design platforms

faqs.question: Is Vectr free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Vectr offers a free plan with limited storage and ads. Paid Plus and Premium plans, starting around 4 dollars per month, remove some limits and add AI credits.

faqs.question: Who owns Vectr?
faqs.answer: Vectr was originally built by Vectr Labs, founded around 2014, and was acquired by stock media company 123RF in 2017. It now operates under Pixlr Pte Ltd, part of Singapore's Inmagine Group.

faqs.question: What file formats does Vectr export?
faqs.answer: Vectr exports designs as SVG, PNG, and JPG, in addition to its native Vectr project format.

faqs.question: Can multiple people edit a Vectr design at once?
faqs.answer: Yes, Vectr supports real-time multi-user collaboration with an integrated chat feature.

faqs.question: Does Vectr have AI features?
faqs.answer: Yes, Vectr includes an AI logo maker, image-to-vector conversion, background removal, text-to-vector generation, and other generative AI tools, metered by AI credits on paid plans.

faqs.question: Is Vectr good for professional illustration work?
faqs.answer: Vectr is best suited for simple vector graphics, logos, and icons. It is not a replacement for professional illustration tools like Adobe Illustrator for complex artwork.

faqs.question: Does Vectr work on desktop?
faqs.answer: Vectr was originally available as a downloadable desktop app in addition to the browser version, with projects syncing across both.

use_cases.title: Logo Design for Small Businesses
use_cases.description: Small business owners and freelancers use Vectr's AI logo maker and vector tools to design simple logos without hiring a designer.

use_cases.title: Collaborative Icon and Asset Creation
use_cases.description: Small design teams use Vectr's real-time collaboration to co-edit icons and simple graphics together in the browser.

use_cases.title: Raster-to-Vector Conversion
use_cases.description: Users convert existing raster logos or sketches into clean, scalable SVG vector files using Vectr's image-to-vector tool.
use_cases.audience: Designers needing scalable versions of existing artwork

enrichment.short_description: VEED is a browser-based AI video editing and creation platform that lets teams edit footage, add subtitles, generate AI avatars, and translate videos for social media and marketing.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (paid plans from about $12/month, billed annually)
enrichment.seo_meta_description: VEED is a browser-based AI video editor with auto-subtitles, AI avatars, and translation tools. Compare VEED pricing plans and key features.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: VEED, also styled VEED.IO, is a browser-based video editing and creation platform that combines a traditional timeline editor with AI-powered tools such as automatic subtitling, AI avatars with voice cloning, background removal, and video translation. It was founded in London in 2018 and bootstrapped its growth without outside funding until February 2022, when it raised a 35 million dollar Series A round led solely by Sequoia Capital, a notable deal because Sequoia was the sole investor in the round. VEED is headquartered in London, United Kingdom, and has grown to roughly 190 to 210 employees spread across Europe, Asia, and North America. As of 2026, third-party estimates put VEED's annual recurring revenue at around 50 million dollars and its valuation at roughly 160 million dollars. The platform is aimed at marketers, content creators, and businesses that need to produce social-ready video without dedicated video editing software or a production team; its customer base reportedly includes large brands such as Amazon, Google, Netflix, Meta, and the BBC using it for internal or marketing video content. Core features include automatic subtitle generation and styling, a library of platform-specific templates for TikTok, Instagram, and YouTube, screen and webcam recording, AI avatars with lip-sync and voice cloning, text-to-video generation from prompts and scripts, background noise reduction, and a brand kit for applying consistent colors, fonts, and logos across videos. VEED offers a free tier with watermarked, lower-resolution exports and short video length limits, and paid tiers, commonly named Lite/Basic, Pro, and Business/Studio, priced from roughly 12 to 70 dollars per user per month depending on billing cycle and resolution/length limits, plus a custom-priced Enterprise tier for larger teams needing 4K exports and unlimited length. VEED competes with other browser-based video tools such as Descript, Kapwing, and CapCut, and, within this directory's later entries, Vidnoz and Vizard, differentiating itself with a broad AI feature set bundled into a single editor rather than a narrower single-purpose tool.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is VEED
paragraphs: VEED is an online, browser-based video editing platform built to let non-specialists produce polished video content quickly, without installing desktop software or hiring a video editor. It combines a standard drag-and-drop timeline editor with a growing set of AI-powered features.

The company was founded in London in 2018 and grew for several years without external funding before raising a 35 million dollar Series A from Sequoia Capital in February 2022, one of relatively few rounds where Sequoia was the sole investor. VEED remains headquartered in London with a distributed team across several continents.

VEED is widely used by social media marketers, content creators, and internal communications teams at both startups and large enterprises, with customers reportedly including Amazon, Google, Netflix, Meta, and the BBC for internal and marketing video production.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: VEED automatically transcribes spoken audio into styled, customizable subtitles, and offers translation and dubbing tools that convert a video's spoken language and captions into other languages for international audiences.

The platform includes AI avatars with lip-sync and voice cloning, allowing users to generate presenter-style videos from a script without filming, alongside text-to-video tools that turn prompts or documents into short video content.

A brand kit feature lets teams save colors, fonts, logos, and a consistent voice across every video, while a library of platform-specific templates helps creators quickly format content for TikTok, Instagram Reels, and YouTube Shorts.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: VEED offers a free plan with watermarked exports, capped resolution, and limits on video length and monthly subtitle minutes, suitable for testing the platform or very light use.

Paid plans are priced per editor and are cheaper when billed annually; entry-level Lite or Basic plans start around 12 dollars per user per month on annual billing, Pro plans run roughly 24 to 55 dollars per user per month depending on the source and billing cycle, and higher Business or Studio tiers can reach around 70 dollars per user per month, with a custom-priced Enterprise tier for large teams needing 4K export, unlimited video length, and dedicated support.

features.title: AI Auto-Subtitles
features.description: Automatically transcribes and styles subtitles for videos in multiple languages.

features.title: AI Avatars and Voice Cloning
features.description: Generates presenter-style videos with synthetic avatars and cloned voices from a script.

features.title: Video Translation and Dubbing
features.description: Translates spoken audio and captions into other languages for global audiences.

features.title: Text-to-Video Generation
features.description: Creates short videos from text prompts or scripts using AI.

features.title: Brand Kit
features.description: Stores brand colors, fonts, logos, and voice for consistent output across videos.

features.title: Platform Templates
features.description: Provides ready-made templates sized for TikTok, Instagram, and YouTube.

features.title: Screen and Webcam Recording
features.description: Records screen and webcam footage directly inside the browser editor.

features.title: Background Removal and Noise Reduction
features.description: Cleans up video and audio quality without third-party plugins.

pros: Broad set of AI features bundled into one browser-based editor
No software installation required, works entirely online
Strong subtitle and translation tools popular with social content creators
Actively funded and developed, with frequent new AI feature releases
Templates make it fast to produce platform-specific social video
cons: Per-editor pricing can get expensive for larger teams
Free plan is limited by watermark, resolution, and length caps
AI features are metered by credits on some plans, adding complexity to cost planning
Browser-based editing can be less responsive than desktop software for very long or complex projects

faqs.question: What is VEED used for?
faqs.answer: VEED is used to edit and create videos for marketing, social media, and internal communications, with AI tools for subtitles, avatars, translation, and text-to-video generation.

faqs.question: Is VEED free?
faqs.answer: VEED offers a free plan with watermarked, lower-resolution exports and length limits. Paid plans starting around 12 dollars per user per month remove the watermark and raise limits.

faqs.question: Who founded VEED?
faqs.answer: VEED was founded in London in 2018 and bootstrapped for several years before raising a 35 million dollar Series A from Sequoia Capital in 2022.

faqs.question: Does VEED offer AI avatars?
faqs.answer: Yes, VEED includes AI avatars with lip-sync and voice cloning that can turn a script into a presenter-style video without filming.

faqs.question: Can VEED translate videos into other languages?
faqs.answer: Yes, VEED offers video translation and dubbing tools that convert spoken audio and captions into multiple languages.

faqs.question: Where is VEED headquartered?
faqs.answer: VEED is headquartered in London, United Kingdom, with a distributed team across Europe, Asia, and North America.

faqs.question: How is VEED different from Vidnoz or Vizard?
faqs.answer: VEED is a general-purpose AI video editor covering subtitles, avatars, translation, and templates in one platform, while Vidnoz and Vizard, covered elsewhere in this directory, focus on narrower use cases such as AI avatar generation or short-clip repurposing from long-form video.

use_cases.title: Social Media Content Creation
use_cases.description: Creators and marketers use VEED's templates and auto-subtitles to quickly produce short-form video for TikTok, Instagram, and YouTube.
use_cases.audience: Social media marketers and content creators

use_cases.title: Internal Corporate Communications
use_cases.description: Enterprises use VEED's AI avatars and screen recording to produce training and internal announcement videos without a production team.
use_cases.audience: Corporate communications and learning and development teams

use_cases.title: Multilingual Video Localization
use_cases.description: Global brands use VEED's translation and dubbing tools to adapt marketing videos for international markets.

enrichment.short_description: Velociraptor is a free, open-source digital forensics and incident response tool for endpoint monitoring, evidence collection, and threat hunting across large fleets of machines.
enrichment.company_size: Open-source project maintained by Rapid7; Rapid7 has 2,000+ employees
enrichment.seo_title: Velociraptor: Open-Source DFIR and Endpoint Monitoring Tool
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Velociraptor is a free, open-source digital forensics and incident response tool for endpoint monitoring and threat hunting, maintained by Rapid7.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Velociraptor is a free, open-source endpoint monitoring, digital forensics, and incident response platform used by security teams to collect forensic evidence, continuously monitor endpoint activity, and hunt for threats across large numbers of machines simultaneously. It was created in 2018 by Mike Cohen, an information security specialist who previously worked on digital and memory forensics at the Australian Department of Defence and the Australian Federal Police, and who spent eight years at Google building related forensic tools including GRR and Rekall. Velociraptor grew out of that experience into an independent, community-driven open-source project built around a flexible query language called VQL, the Velociraptor Query Language, which lets responders write custom artifacts to collect very specific forensic data, such as file system metadata, registry entries, process execution history, and event logs, from thousands of endpoints at once. In April 2021, Rapid7, the publicly traded security company best known for products like InsightIDR and Metasploit, acquired Velociraptor. Rapid7 stated at the time that it had no plans to turn Velociraptor into a paid commercial product and would instead continue investing in the open-source community while also drawing on the technology to strengthen its own incident response offerings, and the tool has remained free and open source since the acquisition. Velociraptor is organized around three core capabilities: targeted collection, quickly pulling specific artifacts from one or many hosts; continuous monitoring, streaming endpoint events like file changes and process launches to a central server for historical analysis; and proactive threat hunting, running a library of pre-built and custom forensic artifacts across a fleet to search for indicators of compromise. Because it can query thousands of endpoints in minutes and store results centrally, it is popular with incident responders, threat hunters, and managed detection and response providers as an alternative or complement to commercial EDR platforms, competing loosely with open-source and commercial DFIR tooling such as GRR, Osquery, and various vendor incident response suites. Security teams are advised to keep it updated, since several CVEs, including remote code execution issues, have been disclosed and patched in past releases.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Velociraptor
paragraphs: Velociraptor is an open-source digital forensics, incident response, and endpoint monitoring tool used by security teams to investigate and hunt for threats across large numbers of computers at once. It is designed for incident responders who need to quickly pull very specific forensic evidence from many endpoints without deploying a full commercial EDR agent.

The project was created in 2018 by Mike Cohen, a forensics specialist with a background at the Australian Department of Defence, the Australian Federal Police, and eight years at Google, where he worked on related open-source forensic tools such as GRR and Rekall. Velociraptor built on those lessons with a more flexible, purpose-built query language for endpoint investigation.

In April 2021, cybersecurity company Rapid7 acquired Velociraptor along with its open-source community. Rapid7 committed to keeping the project open source rather than converting it into a paid product, while using the underlying technology to strengthen its own incident response capabilities.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: At the core of Velociraptor is VQL, the Velociraptor Query Language, a SQL-like language used to write custom artifacts that define exactly what evidence to collect from an endpoint, from specific registry keys to running processes to browser history.

Velociraptor supports simultaneous evidence collection and threat hunting across thousands of endpoints from a single server, returning results in minutes rather than the hours or days a manual, host-by-host investigation would take.

Continuous endpoint monitoring streams events such as file modifications, process execution, and event logs to a central Velociraptor server for indefinite historical storage, letting analysts retroactively investigate activity that occurred before an incident was detected.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Velociraptor is entirely free and open source, with no paid tier or license fee for the software itself, distributed via GitHub under an open-source license.

Organizations pay only for the infrastructure they run the Velociraptor server and agents on, or for professional services and incident response engagements from Rapid7 or other security consultancies that use Velociraptor as part of a paid engagement.

features.title: VQL Query Language
features.description: A flexible, SQL-like language for writing custom forensic artifacts and queries.

features.title: Fleet-Wide Collection
features.description: Collects targeted evidence from thousands of endpoints simultaneously.

features.title: Continuous Monitoring
features.description: Streams endpoint events like file changes and process launches to a central server for historical analysis.

features.title: Threat Hunting Library
features.description: Ships with a large library of pre-built forensic artifacts for common threat-hunting scenarios.

features.title: Cross-Platform Agents
features.description: Supports Windows, macOS, and Linux endpoints from a single management server.

features.title: Offline Collector
features.description: Can generate standalone collector binaries for air-gapped or offline forensic collection.

features.title: Open Source and Auditable
features.description: Fully open-source codebase that security teams can audit and extend themselves.

pros: Completely free, with no licensing cost even for large fleets
Extremely flexible querying via VQL, tailored to specific investigation needs
Scales to thousands of endpoints for rapid fleet-wide investigation
Backed by Rapid7's engineering resources while remaining open source
Strong reputation and adoption within the DFIR community
cons: Steeper learning curve than commercial EDR tools with polished interfaces
Requires teams to self-host and maintain the server infrastructure
Past CVEs, including remote code execution issues, mean patching cadence must be taken seriously
Lacks the built-in threat intelligence and automated response features of full commercial EDR and XDR platforms

faqs.question: What is Velociraptor used for?
faqs.answer: Velociraptor is used for digital forensics, incident response, endpoint monitoring, and threat hunting across large numbers of computers, letting security teams collect evidence and investigate incidents quickly.

faqs.question: Is Velociraptor free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Velociraptor is a free, open-source tool with no license fees, distributed on GitHub.

faqs.question: Who created Velociraptor?
faqs.answer: Velociraptor was created in 2018 by Mike Cohen, a digital forensics specialist who previously worked at the Australian Department of Defence, the Australian Federal Police, and Google.

faqs.question: Who owns Velociraptor now?
faqs.answer: Rapid7, a publicly traded cybersecurity company, acquired Velociraptor in April 2021 and continues to maintain it as an open-source project.

faqs.question: What is VQL in Velociraptor?
faqs.answer: VQL, the Velociraptor Query Language, is the SQL-like language used to write custom artifacts that define what forensic evidence Velociraptor collects from an endpoint.

faqs.question: Does Velociraptor work across large environments?
faqs.answer: Yes, Velociraptor is designed to query and collect evidence from thousands of endpoints simultaneously, returning results in minutes.

faqs.question: Is Velociraptor a replacement for commercial EDR?
faqs.answer: Velociraptor can complement or partially substitute for commercial EDR and XDR tools for forensic collection and threat hunting, but it lacks some of the automated response and built-in threat intelligence features of full commercial platforms.

use_cases.title: Incident Response Investigations
use_cases.description: Incident response teams deploy Velociraptor to rapidly collect forensic artifacts from affected endpoints during an active security incident.

use_cases.title: Proactive Threat Hunting
use_cases.description: Security teams run Velociraptor's artifact library across their fleet on a scheduled basis to search for indicators of compromise before an incident is reported.

use_cases.title: Managed Detection and Response Services
use_cases.description: Managed detection and response providers use Velociraptor as a lightweight, scalable collection and monitoring layer across client environments.
use_cases.audience: MDR and managed security service providers

features: Community support via GitHub and Discord

enrichment.short_description: Veloren is a free, open-source multiplayer voxel action-RPG inspired by Cube World, Minecraft, and Dwarf Fortress, built in Rust by a volunteer community.
enrichment.company_size: Volunteer open-source community, approximately 20 active developers and 150+ contributors
enrichment.seo_title: Veloren: Free Open-Source Voxel RPG Game
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Veloren is a free, open-source multiplayer voxel action-RPG built in Rust, inspired by Cube World and Minecraft, with crafting, dungeons, and mounts.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Veloren is a free, open-source, multiplayer voxel action role-playing game developed entirely by a volunteer community rather than a commercial studio. Development began on May 28, 2018, and the game draws explicit inspiration from Cube World, Minecraft, The Legend of Zelda: Breath of the Wild, and Dwarf Fortress, combining procedurally generated open-world exploration with dynamic combat, crafting, and cooperative multiplayer. Veloren is built with a custom voxel engine written in Rust, structured as a set of modular crates that allow the community to compile and reuse individual engine components independently, and the project is licensed under the GNU General Public License version 3, meaning both its code and its original, community-made graphics, music, and assets are freely available and modifiable. Unlike commercial games, Veloren has no publisher or company behind it; it is coordinated through the VelorenProject organization on GitLab and GitHub by roughly 20 active core developers alongside another 20 or so artists, writers, designers, and composers, with more than 150 total contributors credited over the project's history. Players can explore a fully simulated, procedurally generated fantasy world containing mountains, deserts, and jungles, engage in real-time combat with a variety of weapon types, craft equipment in towns, delve into dungeons with bosses, mine underground cave networks, and tame creatures as companions or mounts, either solo, on public multiplayer servers, or on self-hosted LAN servers. Because it is entirely free and open source with no monetization or in-app purchases of any kind, Veloren is frequently cited in comparisons of free and open-source alternatives to commercial voxel sandbox and survival RPG games, alongside other community-driven titles, and its regular development blog posts and active GitLab repository reflect a still-evolving, pre-1.0 project rather than a finished commercial release.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Veloren
paragraphs: Veloren is a free, open-source, multiplayer voxel role-playing game built and maintained entirely by a volunteer community. It combines open-world exploration, real-time combat, crafting, and cooperative multiplayer in a procedurally generated fantasy setting.

Development began on May 28, 2018, drawing inspiration from games such as Cube World, Minecraft, The Legend of Zelda: Breath of the Wild, and Dwarf Fortress. Unlike those titles, Veloren has no commercial publisher; it is developed in the open on GitLab and GitHub under the GNU General Public License version 3, meaning the code, artwork, music, and other assets are all freely available and modifiable.

The project is coordinated by roughly 20 active core developers plus around 20 additional artists, writers, and composers, with more than 150 total contributors credited across its history. It remains under active, pre-1.0 development, with regular development blog updates.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Veloren's world is procedurally generated and fully simulated, spanning mountains, deserts, jungles, and other biomes that players can explore on foot or by taming and riding creatures as mounts.
Combat is dynamic and weapon-based, supporting multiple playstyles, while towns let players interact with NPCs and craft equipment, and dungeons offer boss encounters and loot for cooperative play.
Players can also mine underground cave networks for resources, and the game supports both public multiplayer servers and self-hosted LAN servers for private play with friends.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Veloren is completely free to download and play, with no purchase price, subscription, or in-app purchases of any kind, consistent with its status as a community-run open-source project rather than a commercial game.
Because the game and its assets are open source under GPL-3.0, players can also build and run their own custom servers or modify the client without paying any licensing fee.

features.title: Procedurally Generated World
features.description: A fully simulated open world spanning multiple biomes, generated algorithmically rather than hand-crafted.

features.title: Dynamic Weapon-Based Combat
features.description: Real-time combat supporting multiple weapon types and playstyles.

features.title: Crafting System
features.description: Players craft weapons, armor, and tools using resources gathered in the world.

features.title: Dungeons and Bosses
features.description: Explorable dungeons with boss encounters and loot.

features.title: Mountable Creatures
features.description: Tame wild creatures to use as companions or mounts.

features.title: Multiplayer and LAN Hosting
features.description: Play on public servers or host private LAN games with friends.

features.title: Open-Source Rust Engine
features.description: Built on a custom, modular voxel engine written in Rust, fully open to community contribution.

pros: Completely free with no purchase price or in-app purchases
Fully open source, so players can inspect, modify, and self-host the game
Active volunteer development community with regular updates
Combines exploration, combat, and crafting reminiscent of beloved titles like Cube World
Supports both public multiplayer and private LAN hosting
cons: Still pre-1.0 and under active development, so content and polish are less complete than commercial competitors
Smaller player base and community than major commercial voxel games like Minecraft
Reliant on volunteer contributors, so development pace can be inconsistent
Voxel art style and performance may not match dedicated commercial game studios' output

faqs.question: Is Veloren free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Veloren is completely free to download and play, with no purchase price, subscriptions, or in-app purchases.

faqs.question: Who made Veloren?
faqs.answer: Veloren is made by a volunteer, open-source community; development began on May 28, 2018, and it has no commercial publisher.

faqs.question: What games is Veloren similar to?
faqs.answer: Veloren is inspired by Cube World, Minecraft, The Legend of Zelda: Breath of the Wild, and Dwarf Fortress, blending voxel exploration, combat, and crafting.

faqs.question: Is Veloren open source?
faqs.answer: Yes, Veloren's code and community-made assets are licensed under the GNU General Public License version 3.

faqs.question: What programming language is Veloren built in?
faqs.answer: Veloren uses a custom voxel game engine written in Rust, organized as modular crates.

faqs.question: Can I host my own Veloren server?
faqs.answer: Yes, Veloren supports both public multiplayer servers and self-hosted LAN servers.

faqs.question: Is Veloren finished?
faqs.answer: No, Veloren is still in active, pre-1.0 development, with the community regularly releasing updates and new content.

use_cases.title: Free Cooperative Voxel RPG Gaming
use_cases.description: Groups of friends play Veloren together on public or self-hosted servers as a free alternative to commercial voxel RPGs.

use_cases.title: Open-Source Game Development Learning
use_cases.description: Rust developers study and contribute to Veloren's engine to learn game engine architecture in a real, active open-source codebase.

use_cases.title: Community-Run Private Servers
use_cases.description: Communities and clans host their own Veloren LAN or dedicated servers for private, customized play.

enrichment.short_description: Vendr is a SaaS buying and software pricing intelligence platform that helps finance and procurement teams benchmark prices and negotiate software contracts; it was acquired by Vertice in 2026.
enrichment.seo_title: Vendr: SaaS Procurement and Pricing Intelligence Platform
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Vendr helps finance and procurement teams benchmark software prices and negotiate SaaS contracts using data from real B2B transactions.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Vendr is a SaaS buying and software pricing intelligence platform used by finance and procurement teams to benchmark what they should pay for software and to negotiate better terms on new purchases and renewals. The company was started in 2018 and went through Y Combinator's Summer 2019 batch before raising significant venture funding, including a 60 million dollar Series A led by Tiger Global and a 150 million dollar Series B co-led by Craft Ventures and SoftBank Vision Fund, bringing total funding to more than 216 million dollars. Vendr is headquartered in Boston, Massachusetts, with a second office in Charleston, and employs roughly 225 people. Its platform is built on a large proprietary dataset, described by the company as more than 15 billion dollars in verified software spend and over 130,000 real negotiations across more than 4,500 suppliers, which it uses to show buyers fair market pricing before they negotiate. Vendr's product evolved from an initial buyer-side negotiation service, where Vendr's own team negotiated software contracts on behalf of customers, into a broader platform called Vendr 2.0 launched in 2022, and more recently into an AI-driven offering with a Pricing Intelligence Agent that surfaces market benchmarks and a Negotiation Agent that can conduct supplier email negotiations with reported success rates around 87 percent. The company reports over 1.4 billion dollars in cumulative customer savings and more than 5,000 finance and procurement teams as users, with reported 2024 annual recurring revenue of around 94.8 million dollars and a valuation near 1 billion dollars. In a significant industry consolidation, Vendr was acquired by Vertice, a competing SaaS spend management platform, effective June 1, 2026, combining the two companies' software pricing benchmarking and negotiation capabilities under one roof. Vendr's pricing for its own platform is not publicly listed and is sold through custom quotes based on a customer's software spend under management, similar to peers such as Zylo and Tropic in the SaaS spend management category.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Vendr
paragraphs: Vendr is a software procurement platform that helps companies figure out what they should be paying for SaaS products and negotiate better deals on new purchases and renewals. It was built around the insight that most companies overpay for software because they lack visibility into what similar buyers actually pay.

The company was started in 2018 and went through Y Combinator's Summer 2019 cohort. It raised a series of venture rounds, including a 60 million dollar Series A led by Tiger Global and a 150 million dollar Series B co-led by Craft Ventures and SoftBank Vision Fund, for total funding above 216 million dollars, and is headquartered in Boston, Massachusetts.

Vendr's original product had its own team of negotiators buy software on behalf of customers directly. The company later broadened into a self-serve software platform, Vendr 2.0, launched in 2022, and more recently layered in AI agents for pricing benchmarking and supplier negotiation.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Vendr's Pricing Intelligence Agent gives buyers real-time benchmark pricing for a given software product based on the company's dataset of more than 15 billion dollars in verified transactions and over 130,000 negotiations, so buyers know what a fair price looks like before entering a negotiation.

The Negotiation Agent can autonomously handle supplier email negotiations, managing counteroffers and terms without requiring buyers to sit in on every call, which Vendr reports achieves roughly an 87 percent success rate across its negotiations.
The platform also includes contract analysis to flag overcharges and negotiable terms in existing agreements, plus integrations with procurement and finance workflows through its API.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Vendr does not publish self-service pricing for its own platform; access is sold through custom quotes typically scoped to a customer's total software spend under management, consistent with how similar SaaS spend management platforms like Tropic and Zylo price their services.

Vendr has historically also offered negotiation-as-a-service engagements, where its team directly negotiates specific software contracts on a customer's behalf, in addition to platform-only subscription access.

features.title: Pricing Intelligence Agent
features.description: Surfaces AI-driven, real-time benchmark pricing for software products based on real transaction data.

features.title: Negotiation Agent
features.description: Automates supplier email negotiations for software contracts and renewals.

features.title: Contract Analysis
features.description: Reviews existing software agreements to identify overcharges and negotiable terms.

features.title: Verified Transaction Dataset
features.description: Draws on more than 15 billion dollars in verified B2B software spend and 130,000+ negotiations.

features.title: Renewal Tracking
features.description: Tracks upcoming software renewals to give procurement teams lead time to negotiate.

features.title: API and Integrations
features.description: Connects Vendr's data and workflows into existing procurement and finance systems.

features.title: SOC 2 Type II Compliance
features.description: Certified for security compliance, relevant to enterprise procurement teams evaluating vendors.

pros: Large proprietary dataset of real software transactions gives credible pricing benchmarks
AI negotiation agent can save procurement teams significant manual negotiation time
Reports substantial cumulative customer savings across thousands of teams
Backed by major investors including Tiger Global, Craft Ventures, and SoftBank
Now combined with Vertice's platform following the 2026 acquisition, potentially broadening capabilities
cons: Platform pricing is not public and requires a custom sales conversation
Best suited to companies with meaningful SaaS spend rather than very small teams
Ongoing integration following the Vertice acquisition may create product changes for existing customers
AI negotiation still requires oversight for complex or strategic vendor relationships

faqs.question: What does Vendr do?
faqs.answer: Vendr is a SaaS buying and pricing intelligence platform that helps finance and procurement teams benchmark software prices and negotiate better contract terms.

faqs.question: When was Vendr founded?
faqs.answer: Vendr was started in 2018 and went through Y Combinator's Summer 2019 batch.

faqs.question: Where is Vendr headquartered?
faqs.answer: Vendr is headquartered in Boston, Massachusetts, with a second office in Charleston.

faqs.question: Has Vendr been acquired?
faqs.answer: Yes, Vendr was acquired by Vertice, a competing SaaS spend management platform, effective June 1, 2026.

faqs.question: How much does Vendr cost?
faqs.answer: Vendr does not publish self-service pricing; access is sold through custom quotes typically based on a company's total software spend under management.

faqs.question: What data does Vendr use for pricing benchmarks?
faqs.answer: Vendr draws on a proprietary dataset described as more than 15 billion dollars in verified software transactions and over 130,000 real negotiations across more than 4,500 suppliers.

faqs.question: Who are Vendr's competitors?
faqs.answer: Vendr competes with SaaS spend management and procurement platforms such as Tropic, Zylo, and its acquirer, Vertice.

use_cases.title: Software Purchase Benchmarking
use_cases.description: Finance and procurement teams use Vendr to see what comparable companies pay for a given software product before starting negotiations.

use_cases.title: Contract Renewal Negotiation
use_cases.description: Companies use Vendr's negotiation tools and agents to secure better pricing and terms on upcoming SaaS contract renewals.
use_cases.audience: IT asset management and procurement teams

use_cases.title: SaaS Spend Consolidation and Oversight
use_cases.description: Finance leaders use Vendr to centralize visibility into software contracts, spend, and renewal timing across the organization.

features: Access to verified transaction benchmark data
